courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Introduction to Architecture
courses.description: ARCH1001 explores creative process, the realms of imagination, and design practices in viewing, experiencing, reading, and ultimately producing architectural FORM. Skills of ideation, representation and fabrication are developed in the context of each student’s capacity to observe, interpret and translate design concepts into physical form.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon successful completion of this course, students will be able to:

| **1. Demonstrate Foundational Technical Control and Perceptual Skills.** | Through a curated portfolio containing orthographic projections (plan, section), paraline drawings (axonometric, plan oblique), and tonal studies (illuminated-volume drawings) that adhere to conventional architectural graphic standards. |
| --- | --- |
| **2. Generate Architectural Form and Spatial Propositions Through Iterative Making.** | By producing a series of developmental sketch models and corresponding drawings that explore multiple spatial solutions from a given set of constraints (e.g., the "Eisenman" exercise). |
| **3. Analyze and Articulate Spatial Conditions Through Critical Drawing Techniques.** | Through analytical drawings—including serial sections, exploded axonometrics, and conceptual (parti) diagrams—that deconstruct and explain the formal, spatial, and experiential logic of both found and invented environments. |
| **4. Synthesize Conceptual, Technical, and Contextual Knowledge into a Coherent Design Intervention.** | Through a final project that integrates precedent analysis, principles of human scale and movement, and material tectonics into a resolved architectural proposition. |
| **5. Critically Curate and Present a Design Process as a Structured Architectural Argument** | By assembling and presenting a final portfolio that selectively sequences work from the semester to construct a clear narrative of learning and design thinking. |
courses.course_syllabus: Each “class” is a studio session – a workshop where the specific issues and methods will be worked “hands-on.” Group projects, individual assignments, critiques, discussions, research and studio sessions will provide students an opportunity to work through issues and ideas within a social, cultural and personal context. The works are intended to serve as a basis for learning by observation, experimentation, and discovery.
courses.required_readings:
1) Francis D.K. Ching. Architecture: Form, Space and Order. 2th ed. Wiley, 2014.
2) Paul Laseau. Graphic Thinking for Architects and Designers. Wiley, 2001.
courses.recommended_readings:
1) Rudolf Arnheim. Art and Visual Perception. 2nd ed. University of California Press, 1974.
2) Francis D.K. Ching. Architectural Graphics. 5th ed. Wiley, 2009.

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Understanding Cities

meetings.location: Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G04
Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G04
Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G04

courses.description:
This course introduces fundamental ways of understanding a city by looking at its architecture and physical environment as sites of collective living, production, exchange and consumption. From the production of art to the collection of rubbish, the course aims to explore the architectural and urban dimensions of cities (from material to cultural and symbolic) through salient topics such as city and war, city and power, city and trade, energy, transportation, housing, and entertainment.
Referring to prominent cities in the world including Hong Kong, we will touch on significant urban themes such as local / global, memory / envisioning, development / sustainability.
Topics include: What is a city for? How do cities work? How are cities made and remade? What role do architects and others play in the design, planning, maintenance and imagining of cities?
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ARCH2111.
courses.learning_outcomes:
- a basic understanding of the way cities work in terms of their architectural and physical aspects

- an awareness of central themes of urban culture and their built manifestations

- an ability to discuss and formulate points of view on important urban issues by reference to appropriate cases

- fundamental skills to transfer the issues presented to other cities and cultures
courses.course_syllabus:
This course introduces fundamental ways of understanding a city by looking at its architecture and physical environment as sites of collective living, production, exchange and consumption. Important historical and contemporary examples will be used to explain the city through its layers. From the production of art to the collection of rubbish, the course aims to explore the architectural and urban dimensions of cities (from material to cultural and symbolic) through salient topics such as city and war, city and power, city and trade, energy, circulation (transportation), housing, entertainment (pleasure and sin), etc.

Referring to Hong Kong as well as other prominent cities in the world, we will touch on significant urban issues such as those between local / global, memory / envisioning, development / sustainability. Through lectures, seminars and group discussions, we will consider these topics by asking key questions such as: What is a city for? How do cities work? How are cities made and remade? What role do architects and others play in the design, planning, maintenance and imagining of cities?

This course aims to provide students with: a basic understanding of the way cities work in terms of their architectural and physical aspects; an awareness of central themes of urban culture and their built manifestations; an ability to discuss and formulate points of view on important urban issues by reference to appropriate cases.

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Visual Studies

meetings.location: Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G03
Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G03
Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G03

courses.description:
ARCH1003 is a foundational course in the instrumental language of architecture. Its primary focus is developing technical proficiency, manual craft, and representational clarity through a series of structured exercises. Students will master core conventions—such as line weight, orthographic projection, axonometric analysis, and model construction—to produce precise, communicative, and well-crafted drawings and physical models.

This disciplined practice establishes the necessary groundwork for the parallel design studio. While the act of drawing is inherently a mode of thinking, the emphasis here is on acquiring the essential skills that will enable and elevate that thinking. The course operates on the principle that a robust command of representation is a prerequisite for effective design exploration and communication.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon successful completion of this course, students will be able to:

| **1. Employ observational and analytical drawing techniques to document and interrogate existing conditions.** | Generate a series of freehand sketches, photographs, and collages that move from descriptive recording to analytical dissection, identifying key formal, spatial, and tectonic qualities of a subject. |
| --- | --- |
| **2. Demonstrate technical proficiency in architectural graphic conventions.** | Produce orthographic drawing sets (plans, sections, elevations) that accurately deploy standardized line weights, notations, and sheet composition to clearly delineate spatial boundaries, materiality, and hierarchy. |
| **3. Construct and utilize paraline drawings as a tool for three-dimensional spatial analysis.** | Produce axonometric projections that actively dissect an object or assembly, using drawing techniques like cutaway, explosion, or highlight to reveal interior relationships, structural logic, or construction sequence. |
| **4. Fabricate precise physical models to explore and communicate massing, scale, and assembly.** | Construct a series of study models, evolving from a unitary massing model to a detailed assembly model, demonstrating control of materials, tools, craft, and the intentional use of abstraction to convey specific architectural ideas. |
| **5. Synthesize a range of representational techniques into a coherent visual argument.** | Assemble a final, structured drawing set (combining technical and freehand drawings) and corresponding model photography that presents a single subject through multiple, complementary lenses, demonstrating an integrated understanding of appropriate representational choice, scale, and presentation craft. |
courses.course_syllabus: This foundational course builds essential visual and tactile literacy for architectural communication. Over the course, students progressively master core techniques through focused modules. The sequence begins with observational skills - Phase1: photographic and freehand documentation), establishing a basis for accurate seeing. Phase2 concentrates on technical orthographic drawing, emphasizing line weight, notation, and craft. Analytical skills are developed through axonometric drawing and the introduction of collage as a multi-media representational tool - Phase3. The course culminates in three-dimensional exploration, with students constructing sequential massing and assembly models, and learning professional model photography (Phase4). Each module delivers a specific, assessed output, ensuring the cumulative development of a precise, versatile skill set directly applicable to the parallel design studio.
courses.required_readings:
1. Douglas Cooper, Drawing and perceiving, Van Nostrand Reinhold, 1992
2. Gu Daqing, Design and Visual Perception, China Architecture and Building Press, 2002
3. Frank Ching, Drawing a creative process, Van Nostrand Reinhold, 1990
4. Betty Edwards, Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain, Fontana, 1982
5. Kimon Nicolaides, The Natural Way to Draw, Houghton Mifflin Company Boston, 1941
6. Claudia Betti and Teel Sale, Drawing —A Contemporary Approach, Harcourt Brace Jovanovich College Publication, 1992

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Introduction to Architectural Design I
courses.description:
Introduction to Architectural Design I to II and Architectural Design Studio I to IV (ARCH2111, 2112, 3113, 3114, 4115, 4116) form a sequence of project-based professional courses in architecture.

Studio 1 – MEASURE (ARCH2111) focuses on cultivating a sophisticated understanding of architecture and its broader contextual built environment, beginning with the simple act of measuring. Measuring in this studio is considered the fundamental tool in establishing a direct relationship between our body and the surrounding physical environment. Through measuring, students will be introduced to a series of architectural concepts, such as dimensioning, geometry and proportion, scale, movement and sequence, circulation and accessibility, public and private, morphology, topography, urban fabric and land use, etc.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Understanding of the fundamental concepts of measurement and how they apply to architecture and urban design.
2. Ability to collect and analyse measurements to create scaled drawings and models, improving design representation skills and reflecting a nuanced understanding of architectural configuration and urban context.
3. Ability to use appropriate representational media, including models and hand-drawn diagrams and projections.
4. Ability to present architectural studies in various formats, including performances and films, enhancing storytelling and presentation skills.
5. Ability to critically engage with architectural drawing archives, interpreting design information and addressing issues of scale and representation.
6. Understanding of visual perception principles and the systems of order that inform architectural composition and urban design.
7. Understanding of the design-build process through hands-on experiences.
8. Awareness of the non-linear nature of the design process.
9. Awareness of historical approaches to urban planning and measurement, such as the Nolli Plan, and how these methods can influence contemporary architectural practices.
10. Awareness of the history of urban development in the Central area of Hong Kong and its relationship to current urban form and social dynamics.
11. Awareness of the relationship between human behaviour and the physical environment, emphasising accessibility and inclusivity.
12. Understanding the impact of buildings on the environment and the way in which buildings fit into their context
13. Ability to document the design process effectively, reflecting on decision-making and evolving ideas through sketches and models.
14. Ability to engage in reflective practice, learning from mistakes and refining designs based on feedback and teamwork.
15. Ability to collaborate effectively with peers, sharing skills and perspectives to tackle complex challenges.

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Introduction to Architectural Design II
courses.description:
Introduction to Architectural Design I to II and Architectural Design Studio I to IV (ARCH2111, 2112, 3113, 3114, 4115, 4116) form a sequence of project-based professional courses in architecture.

Studio 2 – PROTOTYPE (ARCH2112) subverts the conventional approach that design begins with a conceptual design idea to be only materialised and performed later; instead it places performance at the heart of design thinking. Throughout the semester, as they iterate between performing and building, they ultimately construct, activate, and film a building whose form is inseparable from the movements and actions it supports.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: ARCH2111.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Understanding the core concepts of prototyping and their role in shaping architectural design and practice.
2. Building up hands-on skills, testing materials, construction methods, and spatial ideas through iterative modelling and making.
3. Learning a variety of representation techniques, including models, hand sketches, and film, to communicate design concepts and processes as they develop.
4. Familiarity with design-build precedents, exploring how prototyping connects to construction and real-world user experience.
5. Gaining practical experience in the design-build cycle by collaborating in teams, adapting to challenges and shifting opportunities on-site.
6. Embracing and learning the experimental, non-linear nature of prototyping, taking risks and learning through discovery during every phase.
7. Developing awareness of material lifecycles, sustainability strategies, and ways to design for future disassembly and reuse.
8. Understanding how user actions and spatial configuration drive adaptability, accessibility, and collective participation.
9. Awareness of environmental impacts, integrating sustainable choices in materials and methods within the project’s unique context.
10. Learning how to document the entire design-build process, reflecting visually and narratively on decisions, evolving ideas, and teamwork.
11. Learning how to collaborate effectively with peers, sharing ideas and skills to overcome complex design and construction challenges.

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Digital Media
courses.description: Digital media introduces architecture students to core digital design and synthesis methods. Students develop essential skills in digital drafting, 3D modelling, techniques for digitization, and learning to move between analog and digitally driven workflows across different media. Students gain technical fluency and an understanding of how digital tools support contemporary architectural thinking and production processes.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ARCH3222
courses.learning_outcomes: Participants who complete this course will have a broad knowledge of various digital technology, design instruments, and techniques that they can use in architectural design. They will have sufficient practical skills allowing them to immediately apply in design studios and other courses.
courses.course_syllabus: Digital design media, methods of communications, and design tools in architecture are the core topics addressed in this course. Digital instruments for drawing, drafting, image processing, 3D modelling and visualization, layout, video, and fabrication are introduced through lectures covering theoretical concepts and practical skill building exercises. These learning outcomes are applied and transferred to problems and situations arising from the Architectural Design Studio.

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Cultural Contexts I
courses.description: This course is an introduction to the history and theory of Western architecture from Classical Greece to contemporary times. It is primarily concerned with four themes: (1) concepts of geometry and proportion, (2) concepts of architectural representation, (3) professionalism and the institutionalization of architecture, and (4) the relationship between architecture and engineering since the 19th century. Students will acquire fundamental knowledge and terms of reference in preparation for the subsequent courses ARCH2323 and ARCH3321 of the architectural history and theory sequence.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ARCH3322
courses.learning_outcomes: Students who completed this course will have a basic knowledge of a general outline of Western architecture, and develop detailed knowledge of some buildings and architects; an ability to formulate critique in writing and in visual presentation; an awareness of how to connect historical and theoretical knowledge to design.
courses.course_syllabus: In this course, students will explore ideas and practices that shape our architectural heritage - from the Greek conception of ideal beauty to the nineteenth-century idea of time and history. This course attempts to understand how, in various times in the past, these ideas manifest themselves in intellectual and technical levels through individual works of art and architecture; and how ideas are connected across places, times, and types of works

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Cultural Contexts II
courses.description: This course stresses the importance of understanding architectural history and theory with respect to culture and context. There is a specific focus on 20th-century and contemporary architects and buildings, a range of key architects’ philosophies and approaches and the invisible forces around architecture. This course also provides theoretical ground for the parallel design studio and examines architectural history and theory through that lens. Students will study architecture with respect to historical continuities and ruptures, as well as explore the unity of forms and diversity of spatial practices.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ARCH4323
courses.learning_outcomes: Students who completed this course will have a general understanding and appreciation of fundamental ideas, movements and key figures and works of modern and contemporary architecture; an awareness of the broader contexts of Modernist history and culture; have good knowledge on selected buildings, architects and related writings through their course works; have a decent ability to interpret and critique works of modern architecture in writing; an awareness of the applicability of architectural history and theory to the tasks of design.
courses.course_syllabus: Outlines the history and theory of modern architecture through significant works of the 20th century as well as some contemporary exemplars. Works of architecture are examined as artifacts of their time, and as designed objects in their own right and for their own qualities. Focus will be on how individual works relate to important manifestoes, critical and imaginative writings, parallel developments in their cultural, and intellectual historical contexts.

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Building Technology I: Materials and Construction
courses.description: Technical knowledge of materials, construction, and structures is vital for any building endeavour. This foundation is rooted in traditional techniques and practical experience, while also adapting to the latest advancements in science and technology. This course aims to inspire students to discover the art of construction as a driving force in design and to recognise that the essence of a building lies in the poetry of its assembly. By expressing materials strategically, choreographing joints and connections, and celebrating structural logic, we can craft spaces that enrich the human experience.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ARCH3423
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Ability to analyse the technical and tectonic implications of material choices and construction methods
2. Ability to compare the possibilities and limitations of different material systems
3. Ability to identify the primary structural systems in the built environment
4. Ability to describe the behaviour of a structure, including how it supports and delivers loads, achieves adequate stiffness, maintains stability, and develops internal forces
5. Ability to use appropriate representational media, such as models, drawings, diagrams, and photographs, to convey information and explain design decisions
6. Ability to work cooperatively with others in a team setting
7. Develop a comprehensive knowledge of building materials, their properties, and construction techniques
8. Familiarise with the characteristics and design considerations of form-active, vector-active, and section-active structural systems
9. Understand the principles of structural behaviour in withstanding gravity and lateral forces
courses.course_syllabus:
TOPIC 1: STRUCTURAL PRINCIPLES AND PRIMARY TYPES OF STRUCTURAL SYSTEMS IN ARCHITECTURE
Understanding basic structural principles, fundamental structural systems (such as form-active, vecto-active, and section-active), and their physical performance characteristics is essential. This allows students to select the most appropriate system for a given architectural design and explore a wider range of design possibilities, creating more innovative, expressive, and efficient buildings.

TOPIC 2: TECHNICAL ISSUES ASSOCIATED WITH MATERIALS AND CONSTRUCTION
This refers to the practical, engineering-focused challenges that arise when selecting, specifying, and working with different building materials and construction methods. It encompasses considerations like structural integrity, load-bearing capacities, durability, and the technical processes involved in assembly and installation. Understanding these technical and tectonic issues is crucial for ensuring the structural safety, functional performance, and constructability of architectural designs.

TOPIC 3: ISSUE OF SUSTAINABILITY AND INNOVATIVE BUILDING TECHNIQUES
This refers to the need for architects to consider the environmental impact and energy efficiency of the materials and construction methods they specify. It involves understanding how innovative building techniques can contribute to more sustainable and energy-saving design solutions. Addressing these issues is crucial for creating buildings with reduced carbon footprint and operational energy demands.

TOPIC 4: BUILDING TECHNOLOGY AND THE CREATION OF SPACE
This speaks to how material selection, construction methods, and the design of the structural systems could work with spatial design to create a certain spatial experience and atmosphere. Addressing these issues is essential for translating the architect's intended experiential qualities of a space into the built environment. The dual technical and creative aspects of architecture include the need to rigorously address material and construction challenges while also masterfully designing meaningful, experiential spaces.

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Architectural Design Studio I
courses.description:
Introduction to Architectural Design I to II and Architectural Design Studio I to IV (ARCH2111, 2112, 3113, 3114, 4115, 4116) form a sequence of project-based professional courses in architecture.

Studio 3: ADAPT (ARCH3113) critically challenges the conventional myth of architectural permanence—the notion that buildings must remain immutable over time. Rather than viewing architectural adaptation as a passive compromise or a functional step backward, this studio reframes it as the most potent form of architectural agency. In an era defined by climate emergency and social fragmentation, the studio moves beyond reactive survival strategies. Instead, it advocates for a proactive practice that synthesizes cultural resonance with ecological intelligence, empowering architects to actively shape a resilient and transformative future.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: ARCH2112.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Understanding of the need to critically review precedents relevant to the function, organization and technological strategy of design proposals.
2. Understanding the cultural, social and intellectual histories, theories and technologies that influence the design of buildings.
3. Knowledge to apply the influence of history and theory on the spatial, social, and technological aspects of architecture.
4. Knowledge to apply appropriate theoretical concepts to studio design projects, demonstrating a reflective and critical approach.
5. Awareness of the theories and methods of inquiry that seek to show the relationship between human behaviour and the physical environment.
6. Understanding of the needs and aspirations of building users.
7. Awareness of the impact of buildings on the environment, and the precepts of sustainable design.
8. Awareness of the way in which buildings fit into their local context.
9. Understanding of the methods of investigation and preparation of the brief for a design project.
10. Ability to use appropriate representational media, such as drawings, models, diagrams, charts, including computer technology, to convey essential design information at each stage of the programming and design process.
11. Understanding of the fundamentals of visual perception and the principles and systems of order that inform two- and three-dimensional design, architectural composition, and urban design. (design-formal ordering systems).
12. Ability to create architectural designs that satisfy both aesthetic and technical requirements.
13. Ability to work cooperatively with others in a team setting. Ability to discuss architectural ideas with non-architects, to listen objectively to their opinions and to consider those opinions in designing.
14. Ability to employ basic methods of data collection and analysis to inform all aspects of the programming and design process.
15. Ability to speak and write effectively on subject matters contained in the professional curriculum in Chinese and / or English.

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Architectural Design Studio II
courses.description:
Introduction to Architectural Design I to II and Architectural Design Studio I to IV (ARCH2111, 2112, 3113, 3114, 4115, 4116) form a sequence of project-based professional courses in architecture.

Studio 4 - MATERIALISE (ARCH3114) reimagines architectural design as a critical process where making is a tool for discovery, exploring how material form is influenced by tools, labor, and socio-economic pressures. The course includes an overseas field study to examine how local climate and resources shape spatial qualities. Each studio section focuses on a specific material foundation aligned with circular economy principles like upcycling and reuse, viewing sustainability as integral to how buildings adapt and reintegrate into material streams.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: ARCH3113.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Ability to develop a conceptual and critical approach to architectural design that integrates aesthetic ambitions with technical requirements, demonstrated through iterative prototyping, material experimentation, and the development of a comprehensive building proposal grounded in site-specific conditions.
2. Ability to respond to natural and built site characteristics by translating observations from the Kyoto field trip into informed design strategies that connect material investigations with programmatic and spatial decisions.
3. Ability to use appropriate representational media—including hand drawings, digital drawings, physical models, diagrams, and photographic documentation—to communicate design intent and iterative development at all stages of the studio process.
4. Understanding of the principles of structural behaviour as applied to material assemblies and their translation into architectural systems, demonstrated through prototyping, connection detailing, and structural modeling.
5. Understanding of the principles, conventions, standards, and applications of construction materials and assemblies, explored through hands-on engagement with a declared material type, forming and assembly operations, and iterative refinement of tectonic systems.
6. Understanding of the fundamentals of visual perception and systems of order as they relate to material expression, spatial composition, and the iterative design process, demonstrated through crafted prototypes and architectural proposals.
7. Awareness of the theories and methods of inquiry that link human behavior to material and spatial conditions, informed by field observations and cultural analysis during the Kyoto study trip.
8. Adequate knowledge to apply theoretical concepts to studio design projects, demonstrating a reflective and critical approach to material agency, cultural context, and iterative design thinking.
9. Knowledge to creatively apply fine arts principles—such as composition, proportion, and visual narrative—to the conceptualization and representation of material-based design investigations and architectural proposals.

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Architecture and Global Culture
courses.description: Architecture and Global Culture is a thematic history and theory course for advanced undergraduates. As the culmination of three-course sequence in architectural history and theory, it seeks to prepare the student to take a position in contemporary debates of a global nature, debates in which architects may ultimately serve a significant role.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ARCH2321
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Improve capacities in deep reading: you will be encouraged to read texts both critically and closely. What do they say, but also, what do they obscure or neglect? What positions do they present? What subtexts do they contain?
* And capacities for scholarly writing: How can you use established scientific genres to communicate your message? Where is the line between description and advocacy?
* Sharpen skills in contextual analysis: At what scale are you arguing? What is the regional/global divide in your argument? How does the Greater Bay Area affect the globe, and vice versa? Who is your present constituency, and who are the future generations who will be shaped by the forces you describe?
* Train historical reasoning: What is the genealogy of motifs that shape discourse today? How are they similar, and how do they differ, between China and the West?
* Identify cross-disciplinary implications: How does architecture intersect with other fields such as environmental science, sociology, politics, ethics and anthropology? Phrased differently: when is a building not a mere construction but a mistake, a social space, a cradle of politics, a crime, or a ritual?
* Practice representational strategies, in particular via architectural drawing.
* Develop critical thinking skills

courses.description: This course introduces the fundamental concepts of passive environmental design. It examines the effect on buildings and their occupants of environmental conditions of light, temperature, air movement and sound. Case studies are used to review both traditional and current approaches of representative building types in more depth.
courses.learning_outcomes: Upon the completion of the course, students will have demonstrated basic understanding of fundamental issues related to the environmental design of architecture. They will acquire knowledge of the subject with sufficient exposure and at a suitable level so that they will be able to appreciate the topical issues of climate, human bio-meteorology and the design of indoor and outdoor environment for human habitation. This will allow the design of better buildings, further study, or to pursue a career in the building design and construction industry.
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduces the fundamental concepts of passive environmental design. Examines the effect on buildings and their occupants of environmental conditions of light, temperature, air movement, and sound. Case studies are used to reviews both traditional and current approaches of representative building types in more depth.

• To understand the significances of environmental design in architecture.
• To understand and to be able to apply appropriate environmental strategies in different climatic and urban contexts to achieve human comfort.
• To have a basic understanding of the building physics related to passive environmental design.
• To appreciate the application and integration of passive environmental design in architectural works.
• To develop skills in conducting a systematic investigation into the subject and presenting the results in a clear and convincing manner.

courses.description:
This course introduces students to key theories and practices in the design of the built environment, emphasizing the reciprocal relationships between urban forms and the socio-economic and environmental processes that shape them. Through lectures, discussions, and local field trips, students will attain a historical perspective on the development of cultural landscapes across diverse geographical settings. Importantly, they will be encouraged to challenge preconceived notions while enhancing their critical thinking and writing skills in architectural and urban research.

The course encompasses three interrelated aspects: (1) ‘Urban Morphology,’ which examines evolving discourses in planning, architecture and urban design; (2) ‘Urban Landscapes,’ focusing on the growing significance of landscape architecture in development; and (3) ‘Urban Sustainability,’ exploring theories and practices related to sustainable cities. Throughout the course, students will engage with emerging sustainability challenges of the 21st century.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ARCH4721
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Acquire fundamental knowledge of the principles of urban planning and design and how these practices have co-evolved with social, cultural, and economic processes from a historical perspective.
* Demonstrate a critical understanding of the complexity and interconnectedness of urban challenges by thinking across times and geographical scales.
* Understand the basic principles of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and connect them with planning and design practices, including the use and management of resources and their environmental and social impacts.
* Become familiar with the intersections among various built environment disciplines, including planning, architecture, landscape architecture, and urban design.
* Acquire knowledge of Hong Kong’s urban histories and their relationship with local, regional, and international development.
* Develop critical thinking and analytical skills essential for architectural and urban research.
courses.course_syllabus: This course offers a comprehensive introduction to major intellectual currents that underpin of contemporary planning and urban design, as well as the socio-economic and environmental processes that have shaped cultural landscapes across diverse geographical settings. Emphasis will be placed on connecting theory and practice through in-depth analysis of international and local case studies. The aim is to equip students with a critical understanding of the complex dynamics influencing the forms of the built environment while encouraging reflection on the multifaceted challenges faced by design professionals in historical and contemporary contexts.

courses.description:
Introduction to Architectural Design I to II and Architectural Design Studio I to IV (ARCH2111, 2112, 3113, 3114, 4115, 4116) form a sequence of project-based professional courses in architecture.

Studio 5 - INHABIT (ARCH4115) defines "inhabiting" as a process where dwellers adapt and create meaning within their environment, focusing on the link between inhabitants and the poetic aspects of daily life. The course reframes affordable housing as an architectural strategy aimed at restoring dignity to individuals with limited economic resources. It explores various models and encourages innovation in development and resource conservation. The studio emphasizes balancing economic feasibility with design goals, positioning housing as a means to promote social equity and human dignity in a modern context.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: ARCH3114.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To engage with the reading of the city and different urban conditions through disciplinary studies and analysis.
2. To develop a design project with specific programs that foster the community life and individual living, particularly considering the needs of different groups of people.
3. To examine the complexities implicit in resolving urban problems acquiring proficiency in working across different scales: from the city to the building, from building to the living unit.
4. To reflect on the critical challenge of the affordable housing by conceptualizing alternative forms of living, providing a sustainable architectural prototype.

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Architectural Design Studio IV
courses.description:
Introduction to Architectural Design I to II and Architectural Design Studio I to IV (ARCH2111, 2112, 3113, 3114, 4115, 4116) form a sequence of project-based professional courses in architecture.

Studio 6 – PERFORM (ARCH4116) is a Design Thesis. It serves as the final milestone of the undergraduate architectural journey, marking the transition from guided learning to independent inquiry. Students are responsible for defining their own architectural challenges and positions that require the ability to identify a critical contemporary issue, research its background, and develop a unique professional voice.

The Design Thesis is a project of high complexity that requires translating abstract theories into a solid, coherent architectural proposal. Students must navigate conflicting forces and layers, treating architecture as a sophisticated argument rather than just a simple building solution. By the end of the semester, students are expected to test their own design philosophy, delivering a deeply considered project that expresses their architectural intentions with clarity and conviction. This final work acts as a rigorous proof of their readiness for the professional world.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: ARCH4115.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Integrated Design Synthesis: Develop a conceptually rigorous architectural project (up to schematic design) that synthesizes program requirements, site context, user needs, aesthetic intent, and technical considerations, demonstrating a critical and reflective design approach.
2. Technical Systems Integration: Understand, assess, select, and integrate structural systems, building envelope systems, and building assemblies into a coherent design solution.
3. Sustainable Design Principles: Selectively incorporate principles of sustainable design, such as low-carbon strategies, and green architecture (encompassing energy, water, materials, indoor environmental quality, and site ecology) to minimize environmental impact and enhance building performance.
4. Human-Centered Design: Understand and address the diverse needs, health, safety, well-being, and aspirations of building users, including designing for accessibility and varying physical abilities.
5. Contextual & Community Responsiveness: Analyze and respond to site characteristics (natural and built), local context, and the potential impact of the project on existing and proposed communities.
6. Program Development & Briefing: Develop well-defined building briefs/programs of targeted scales/types, establishing clear client/user requirements and their appropriateness to site and context.
7. Regulatory & Life Safety Compliance: Understand and apply relevant regulatory requirements, building codes, and the fundamental principles governing the design of life-safety systems and provisions.
8. Effective Representation & Communication: Proficiently prepare, document, and present design proposals using appropriate representational media (drawings, models, diagrams, digital tools) and communicate complex architectural ideas clearly.
9. Critical Evaluation & Analysis: Critically analyze, evaluate, and assess buildings, urban spaces, design proposals, and the completed project against program criteria, technical performance, and contextual fit.
10. Research & Inquiry: Employ appropriate methods of research, data collection, analysis, and theoretical inquiry to inform programming, design decisions, and understanding of human behavior in relation to the physical environment.

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Building Systems Integration
courses.description:
After exposure to material and construction, building structures, and environmental technology, it is time for students to take these systems and considerations to shape their next studio project. Through the appreciation of finely integrated projects and examination of daily surroundings, students are encouraged to rethink the core value of integration to the practice of architectural design and appreciate the technical aspects that inform our design decisions and strengthen our scheme from the inside.

The course “Building Systems Integration” aims to empower students to 1) acquire fundamental understanding towards building systems, 2) investigate system integrations through building information modelling (BIM), and 3) challenge the effectiveness and efficiency of the existing building designs. Through the process, students reflect on how building technologies can be integrated for the articulation of their design concepts and resultant spaces.
courses.course_syllabus: This course is challenge-driven and student-led, as structured by a series of lectures, guest lectures, student presentations, field trips, hands-on workshops and project-based assignments. Project-based assignments include 1) Case studies, which equip students with the foundational knowledge regarding building systems and their integration with architecture, 2) BIM studies, and 3) Redesign proposals. In-class training by BIM expert will be arranged. The level of difficulty increases progressively from Assignment 1 to 3 and will align with studio teaching to maximise the learning outcomes from the students’ perspective. Tutorials will be arranged to have in-depth discussions with students in group formats.

courses.description: This elective course is offered to investigate urban studies. Each specific elective is designed to allow students to gain in-depth knowledge and ability to discourse or execute the theory and practice of the particular field. There will be different number of electives offered with specific course description for students to choose from, designated as 4731A/B/C/D, etc. Students are allowed to take these courses at most twice, provided that the course contents are different, and gain the units each time they pass the course. courses.course_syllabus:
This course will explore ideas on informal architecture and urbanism. In early societies, spatial orders were evolved out of the necessities of daily patterns. People dwelled under shelters, exchanged at crossroads, played on open fields, and defended within walls. Settlements, then cities, were shaped and rebuilt according to prescribed orders of divinity or democracy. Architecture and its extending urbanism, thus, take forms to accommodate, to contain and even to express design ideas. But when forms are substituting spaces, and formalities are replacing meanings, architecture without its proper engagement of the people’s cultures and their associated ethos will become a convention, or simply an art of composition.

In places under the pressure of rapid development, like most Asian cities, where transplantations of formal architecture and urbanism colonize prominent locations, core values of local communities have been diluted. In fact, there is much to be appreciated when we ‘go to the crowds’ and learn from them how to cohabit with the conditions. Recently there has been a social awareness of the local culture and its direct application in design. And if we examine the ways of living and see how they are transforming spaces to be informal but meaningful, our environment will be more colorful and humanized, just like David Adjaye noted, “…A similar notion could be applied to the way that vines work with walls in a symbiotic way; they start to destabilize the wall and eventually the two cohabit. And co-dependency becomes a requirement of this cohabitation.”

In this perspective, we will put forward critical issues on urban phenomenon, organic growth, collective memory, public engagement and community building. Cases will be introduced, students’ initiatives will be encouraged and guest speakers from Asian cities will be invited to confer further ideas.

courses.description: The year-long Design Studio forms the core of the Master of Architecture programme, with each course directly connected with the School’s design, societal and environmental agenda. Students are expected to design an architectural proposal from concept to final representation, through careful consideration to scale, context, tectonics, material and technology. The course offers a wide range of topics including: Urbanism, Building Technology, History & Theory, Digital Design and Design & Practice. It is geared towards developing innovative forms of competence and enabling students to work on different architectural fields, from the design of urban masterplans to individual buildings, from landscape redevelopments to adaptive reuse of existing buildings, from sustainable building designs to application of the latest building technologies.

courses.description: The Design Studio forms the core of the Master of Architecture programme, with each course directly connected with the School’s design, societal and environmental agenda. Students are expected to design an architectural proposal from concept to final representation, through careful consideration to scale, context, tectonics, material and technology. The course offers a wide range of topics including: Urbanism, Building Technology, History & Theory, Digital Design and Design & Practice. It is geared towards developing innovative forms of competence and enabling students to work on different architectural fields, from the design of urban masterplans to individual buildings, from landscape redevelopments to adaptive reuse of existing buildings, from sustainable building designs to application of the latest building technologies.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Architecture
courses.learning_outcomes:
Develop complex architectural propositions, with a rigorous understanding of contextual and environmental issues, contemporary technological challenges, historical and theoretical meanings and programmatic requirements.
Develop the ability to be part of an innovative intellectual climate, promoting design research exploration together with the ability to work collaboratively in teams.
Develop the communication skills related to technical, spatial and conceptual aspects of design through drawings, models as well as verbal communication.
Develop the ability to pursue and develop a design project through research exploration.
Develop a design working methodology that is consistent with professional and ethical standards.
Understand the social responsibilities of design, consistent with the School’s 25 year history.
courses.course_syllabus: This studio course addresses particular issues in architectural design. Part I consists of topic research and site analysis. Part II is the development of a building design proposal that addresses the studio topic or theme.

courses.description: This advanced course introduces an iterative approach to design problem solving using Artificial Intelligence. Students will engage with ethical approaches to AI-driven design methods to explore contemporary and future applications in architectural design practice. The course emphasizes how AI can enhance design agency, optimize workflows, and support critical decision-making in design processes.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Architecture
courses.learning_outcomes: The topics in this course refer to issues in architectural design, theory, and practice methods underpinned by contemporary and emerging use of Artificial Intelligence (AI). The course will provide students with skills to understand complex forces that shape contemporary architectural design, practice, and innovation with digital technology. Students will gain familiarity, literacy, and ethical awareness of AI technologies to help understand technology driven organizational systems and articulate the relationship between design abstraction, physical translation for built environments.
courses.course_syllabus:
01\_Introduction to AI in Architecture
Overview of AI concepts in design.

02\_Computational Design
AI supported algorithmic approaches to design.

03\_Machine Learning and Optimization
Techniques for training AI models and improving design performance.

04\_AI Tools and Platforms
Software and frameworks for design workflows.

05\_AI-Driven Data and Simulation
Using AI to analyze and simulate spatial data.

06\_AI-Enhanced Design Prototyping
Building and testing AI-integrated workflows.

07\_Foundational AI Literature
Key debates shaping AI in architecture.

08\_AI, Bias, and Ethics
Responsible use of AI in design.

09\_Human-AI Collaboration
Co-authorship and agency in creative practice.

10\_Speculative Futures with AI
Long-term impacts of AI on architecture.

courses.title: Architectural Theory and Criticism
courses.description: This is a challenging course designed to re-acquaint master’s students with writing as a reflective practice, as a means of communication, and as a scientific resource. Like drawing, writing is a fundamental tool of architecture. It is also a practice. Both free writing—where one just observes and remarks on experiences—and research writing—where one carefully formulates questions, compiles supporting information, evaluates it, presents a case, and draws conclusions—will be required of students. All tools, from site and archival visits, to critical drawing and AI, are woven into the research examples. LLMs are introduced as a research and interrogation tool, but the use of AI to generate prose is discouraged. Master’s students will be ready to write a dissertation and perhaps even pursue additional post-graduate research with the help of this course.

courses.learning_outcomes:
- Able to analyze critical writing and buildings, and to draw out the ideas behind them.
- Able to discuss major concepts of architectural theory and criticism from the mid twentieth century.
- Able to complement the design studio through the influential projects and ideas discussed in class.
courses.course_syllabus: Topics are Metropolis, Simplicity, Avant Garde, Complexity, Context, Phenomenon, Performance, Representation, Topology and Projective.

courses.description: ARCH 5421 Advanced Building Systems I- Systems-led Design Integration positions technology as a core design agency, directly supporting students’ design studio work. Resonating with ARCH 5422, the course pushes forward the technical workflow established in term 1 and focuses on systems integration as design synthesis, teaching students to cohesively combine structural, envelope, building services and interior systems to advance their architectural concepts. This course also promotes sustainable design strategies by revealing how technical choices could be made during the design process. Students are supported to employ BIM and AI tools to enhance the technical resolution, analysis and representation for their projects, proactively engaging technology into innovative and sustainable spatial creations.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Architecture
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Understand the design articulation relies on our exploration with technical solutions.
2. Be able to formulate the “systems integrating challenge” for your studio project.
3. Be able to model major building systems spatially by digital or physical means.
4. Design building systems with respect to building program and systems’ technical requirements.
5. Recognize the significance of sustainable development and architects' roles in promoting it.
6. Be able to consider barrier-free environments for your studio project.
7. Be able to investigate and select alternative structural, constructional, and material systems relevant to local architectural design practices.
8. Comprehend the physical properties, characteristics, and environmental impact of building materials, components, and systems.
9. Practice integrating building systems involving at least four core components/systems.
10. Create a schematic building program considering site parameters and regulations.
11. Be able to prepare technical documentation meeting industry standards.
12. Acknowledge managerial responsibilities in project management, cost control, legal compliance, building code adherence, and contract management.
courses.course_syllabus:
Building performances and various building services systems are to be discussed in the following topic:
- Structural Systems- driven Design Integration
- Envelope Systems- driven Design Integration
- Interior Systems- driven Design Integration
- Sustainability- driven Design Integration
- Modular Integrated Construction/ Multi-trade integrated Mechanical, Electrical & Plumbing
- Advanced building services systems, e.g. 5 Constants System

1. Rush, Richard D. The Building Systems Integration Handbook. Boston: Butterworth-Heinemann, 1986. Print.

2. Bachman, Leonard R. Integrated Buildings: The Systems Basis of Architecture. New York: J. Wiley & Sons, 2003. Print.

courses.description: This course explores how building performance requirements and systems integration shapes architectural design. Through a structured framework of case studies, and site visits—students learn interdependent relationships between program, requirements, systems, design concepts, and compliance. The curriculum introduces passive to active technological strategies, and encompasses building information modelling and artificial intelligence in design resolutions, to fulfill quality space, statutory requirements, fire safety, and accessibility standards. Students will learn to analyze how technical decisions influence building form, materiality, and space, transforming regulatory frameworks into catalysts, rather than constraints, for innovative, responsible design solutions that answers architectural design intent with systems performance.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Architecture
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Understand the technical workflow and be able to design with reference to it.
2. Understand how human behavior influences building performance requirements and then the building systems design.
3. Recognize the significance of sustainable development and architects' roles in promoting it.
4. Be able to consider barrier-free environments for your studio project.
5. Be able to investigate and select alternative structural, constructional, and material systems relevant to local architectural design practices.
6. Comprehend the physical properties, characteristics, and environmental impact of building materials, components, and systems.
7. Develop a comprehensive understanding of building services systems in the local industry.
8. Gain knowledge of life-safety systems within the local context.
9. Create a schematic building program considering site parameters and regulations.
10. Be able to prepare technical documentation meeting industry standards.
11. Recognize the leadership role of architects within design teams and the construction industry, staying informed about current industry methods and trends.
12. Acknowledge managerial responsibilities in project management, cost control, legal compliance, building code adherence, and contract management.
courses.course_syllabus:
This course employs a **pedagogical matrix** to illustrate the interdependencies among building performances, core systems, and technological approaches. The framework operates as follows:

1. **X-Axis: Design Drivers (Topics)**
1. **Building Performances** (4 Priority Issues):
1. **Comfort**: Thermal, acoustic, spatial quality
2. **Safety**: Structural integrity, fire egress, hazard mitigation
3. **Health**: Indoor air quality, daylighting, biophilic engagement
4. **Energisation**: Energy autonomy, carbon neutrality, grid resilience
2. **Building systems (4 Core systems):**
1. **Structural**: Load-bearing frameworks & seismic strategies
2. **Interior**: Space-defining partitions & finish assemblies
3. **Envelope**: Climate-regulating skins & interfaces
4. **Mechanical**: Active environmental control systems

2. **Y-Axis: Implementation Spectrum (Cases)**
1. **Statutory Baseline**: Establish code minima
2. **Basic Affordable**: Low-tech baseline solutions
3. **Common Practice**: Industry-standard MEP integrations
4. **Advanced Engineered**: Performance-optimized innovations
5. **Cutting-edge Custom**: Bespoke technological synthesis

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Urban History and Theory
courses.description: This course examines the main ideas, histories and theories of the city through specific local and international examples from significant historical, political, geographical and environmental periods and aspects. Important themes and debates in urban design and planning will be critically explored to understand the relationship between the urban forms, the culture of cities and their sustainable development. In particular, the way urban forms have been used and reinterpreted, adapted and challenged by different social, economic and political contexts. Exemplary design projects and planning policies are investigated in view of their relationship to contexts and their impacts on communities and the environment.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Architecture and MSc in Urban Design;Mutually exclusive to URBD5703
courses.learning_outcomes:
The course aims to familiarize students with the histories and theories of urbanism and the role of design in shaping city forms and social environments. It seeks to equip students with knowledge of city planning, design, and development policy, as well as the needs and aspirations of local communities. Through in-class discussions and debates, students are encouraged to challenge their preconceived assumptions and analyse various urban issues from comparative perspectives. The learning process will also help them develop critical thinking and academic writing skills relevant to research in architecture and urban studies.

Students are allowed to use AI tools in some learning activities as specified in the individual assignment handouts. In such cases, they are required to explicitly acknowledge the use of these tools, and must document the specific tools used, how they were employed, their assessment of the feedback received from the tools and whether the feedback was incorporated into their submitted work.
courses.course_syllabus:
The course familiarizes students with various methods for studying cities as pursued in the social sciences and humanities, including history, geography, and urban studies. Through in-depth discussion and debates of selected case studies, students will gain a historical perspective of urbanization and develop the ability to analyze specific urban issues, identifying continuities and congruences in city development across different time periods and locations.

This course aligns with the framework of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and the UN Habitat’s Urban Agenda, emphasizing the importance of interdisciplinary thinking to address today’s pressing urban challenges. A major assignment will require students to explore the concepts of the SDGs and establish meaningful connections with contemporary design practice in Hong Kong.

Term 1: Students should demonstrate ability to understand various research agendas and their relevance to their own research by participating in academic events in their fields.

Term 2: Students should be able to (1) demonstrate the capacity to provide critical feedback on the research of their peers; and (2) organise an academic event in the form of a seminar or a conference.
courses.course_syllabus: Discussion of current research and research traditions in the various domains of architecture.
courses.feedback_evaluation: To be negotiated between the teacher and the student.
courses.required_readings: To be recommended by the supervisor/course instructor
courses.recommended_readings: To be recommended by the supervisor/course instructor

courses.subject: ARCH
courses.title: Research Methods I
courses.description: An introduction to research methods common to the various domains of architecture.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Architecture
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students should

1. Understand the basics of research methods in architecture and its allied environmental disciplines.
2. Able to define the scope of a research thesis and frame relevant research questions.
3. Able to locate different types of sources for a research thesis.
4. Develop a critical intellectual position as required for academic research.
courses.course_syllabus: This course is designed to help students define the scope of their thesis, frame relevant research questions and arguments, and become familiar with the types and usage of reference materials for their thesis projects. Course assignments will enable students to articulate a critical intellectual position through the development of a research prospectus, thereby deepening their understanding of the significant role of research in architecture and the built environment. The course will include lectures, discussions, and other in-class activities.
courses.required_readings: Recommended by the supervisor/course instructor
